payload.input: "Nothing," Maya says, clutching the phone she retrieved from the hedge, now wiped clean and tucked in her bra. "We bring only what we are."  Vivian’s smile widens, genuine and terrible. "Poetic. Julian, the car."  They move in synchronized chaos—Julian leading the way to the Lexus, Vivian’s hand at the small of Maya’s back, propelling her toward the vehicle. Ethan follows, his polished shoes scraping on the concrete, his pomaded hair catching the noon sun as he ducks into the backseat. Maya slides in beside him, the leather seats cold against her bare thighs. Vivian climbs into the front passenger seat, twisting to face them as Julian throws the car into drive. As they pull away from the curb, Maya sees the pastor’s sedan turning the corner, Pastor Jim’s face visible through the windshield, his hand raised in a wave that freezes mid-gesture as he recognizes the children in the back of the stranger’s car. "Wave back," Vivian instructs, her voice soft as velvet. "Smile. Be grateful."  Maya lifts her hand, the silver band catching the light, and smiles her brightest Sunday school smile as the Lexus accelerates past the pastor’s slowing vehicle, leaving him in the rearview mirror with his mouth open and his Bible forgotten on the dash, watching them disappear toward the highway, toward the Marriott, toward the education that awaits.
